metadata.summary: AIDA-driven creative: scroll-stopping visuals, benefit-led copy with hooks, 1:1 and 9:16 formats, systematic testing with variable isolation, refresh every 2-4 weeks on fatigue.

content.title: Meta Creative Best Practices
content.summary: AIDA-driven creative production for Meta Ads: stop the scroll with a strong visual hook (faces, before/after, bold contrast), write benefit-led copy with a tested hook formula (question/claim/curiosity/social-proof/direct), produce both 1:1 and 9:16 format variants, and test creatives systematically by isolating one variable at a time. Refresh every 2-4 weeks when CTR declines or frequency exceeds 3.

why.p: Targeting configuration accounts for roughly 20-30% of ad performance; creative accounts for the rest. Most advertisers spend 90% of their time on targeting and wonder why ads underperform. A single creative change — hook wording or visual type — can shift CTR from 0.8% to 1.5%+. Without a testing framework, every campaign iteration is a guess.

list.item: Briefing or producing new Meta ad creatives (image, video, carousel)
Setting up a creative testing structure with multiple hook/visual variants
Diagnosing low CTR or high CPA when audience and budget are not the constraint
Planning a creative refresh after fatigue signals appear
When budget is under $20-50 per creative — not enough signal to judge performance
When the offer or value proposition is still unclear — creative cannot rescue a confusing product
For Google Display or Search creative — specs and conventions differ significantly

section.name: format-selection
section.p: Format choice determines production cost, iteration speed, and available placements. Start simple and add complexity only after finding a winning angle.

rule.statement: Start with static images, add video once you find a winning angle, use carousel for multi-product or multi-benefit showcases.
rule.rationale: Static images are fastest to produce and test. Video requires more production investment; spending it before knowing the winning angle wastes resources. Carousel works when multiple options increase relevance for different segments.

list.item: Static image: 1080x1080 (1:1) or 1080x1920 (9:16). Best for simple messages and fast iteration.
Video: 15-30 seconds, vertical (9:16) or square (1:1). Best for storytelling, demos, testimonials.
Carousel: 2-10 images, 1080x1080. Best for product catalogs or feature lists.
Stories/Reels: 1080x1920, 15 seconds max. Best for full-screen, native-looking content.

section.name: visual-rules
section.p: Visual impact determines whether the ad is even seen. The human eye and thumb decide in milliseconds.

rule.statement: Apply the 3-second rule: video must hook within 3 seconds; image must communicate the core message within 3 seconds.
rule.rationale: The average user scrolls past an ad in 1.7 seconds on mobile. If the visual does not stop the scroll immediately, no copy or CTA will ever be read.

section.p: What stops the scroll: faces with strong emotion, before/after contrasts, product in action, bold high-contrast colors, text overlays (concise). What fails: stock photos that look obviously staged, busy compositions with multiple competing focal points, low contrast, overly small subject matter.

rule.statement: Always produce both 1:1 and 9:16 format variants for every creative concept.
rule.rationale: Feed placements favor square/portrait; Stories and Reels require 9:16 full-screen. Running a 1:1 creative in Stories shows letterboxing, which signals "this is an ad" and reduces engagement.

section.name: copy-structure
section.p: Copy must lead with benefit and use proven hook formulas. Benefit-led messaging outperforms feature lists consistently.
Primary text character limits: ~125 characters shown before "See more" — hook must land in those first 125. Headline: 40 characters, benefit-focused. Description: 30 characters, optional support.

list.item: Question hook: "Tired of [problem]?" — activates recognition and stops self-identifying prospects
Bold claim: "We increased [metric] by 400%" — credible if the number is specific and defensible
Curiosity: "This simple change doubled our [result]" — creates information gap
Social proof: "10,000+ [audience] are using this" — reduces risk by social validation
Direct benefit: "Get [outcome] in [timeframe]" — high clarity, works for transactional offers

example.title: Benefit-led vs feature-focused copy
example.code:
POOR (feature-focused): "Our software uses AI to analyze your marketing data. Features include dashboard, reports, integrations."
BETTER (benefit-led): "Stop guessing which marketing works.
You're spending $10K+ on ads. But which ones actually drive sales?
[Product] shows you in 60 seconds: - Which campaigns are profitable - Where you're wasting money - What to do next
10,000+ marketers use it daily.
Start free →"

antipattern.description: Opening the primary text with the company name or product name.
antipattern.reason: The first line must be a hook that grabs the reader's attention. Company names convey no value to a cold audience; they are only useful after the hook has earned the reader's attention.

section.name: testing-priority
section.p: Testing budget is finite. Prioritize variables by their impact on performance.

rule.statement: Test in this order: hook first, then visual, then copy details, then format, then CTA.
rule.rationale: Hook has the highest impact on CTR; it determines whether the ad is read at all. Visual determines thumb-stop rate. Copy details and CTA have smaller marginal effects. Testing low-impact elements first wastes budget before finding the highest-leverage change.

rule.statement: Change one variable per test; hold everything else constant.
rule.rationale: If you change hook AND visual simultaneously and performance improves, you cannot attribute the lift to either. Isolated tests produce learnings that compound; compound tests produce confusion.

example.title: Correct testing structure isolating hook variable
example.code:
Ad Set: [Audience — same for all] ├── Ad 1: Hook A "Tired of losing money on ads?" + Visual A ├── Ad 2: Hook B "10x your ROAS in 30 days" + Visual A └── Ad 3: Hook C "What if your ads actually worked?" + Visual A
After finding winner: Ad Set: [Same Audience] ├── Ad 1: Hook Winner + Visual A (control) ├── Ad 2: Hook Winner + Visual B └── Ad 3: Hook Winner + Visual C

section.name: minimum-spend-thresholds
section.p: Data quality depends on sample size. Premature optimization on small samples is worse than no optimization.

rule.statement: Spend $20-50 per creative and accumulate 1,000+ impressions before judging CTR; require 100+ conversions per variant before judging CPA.
rule.rationale: Below these thresholds, variance dominates signal. A 1.2% CTR on 200 impressions has a confidence interval that includes everything from 0.4% to 2.0%. Decisions made below threshold are coin flips presented as data.

section.name: fatigue-signals-and-refresh-cadence
section.p: Creative fatigue is predictable and preventable with disciplined refresh cycles.

rule.statement: Refresh creatives every 2-4 weeks when CTR declines more than 20% week-over-week, CPM rises while CTR falls, or frequency exceeds 3.
rule.rationale: Fatigue means the audience has seen the ad enough times that it no longer triggers attention. The audience is not exhausted — only the creative is. A fresh hook and visual restores performance without requiring audience expansion.

section.p: Refresh strategy: iterate on winners rather than starting from scratch. If a hook worked, write three new variations of the same hook formula. If a visual format worked, produce new content in the same format. Full creative reinvention is slower and riskier than iteration on proven elements.

antipattern.description: Running only one creative per ad set.
antipattern.reason: With a single creative, there is nothing to learn from and no fallback when fatigue hits. Minimum 3-5 creatives per ad set enables testing and prevents account-wide fatigue cascades.

section.name: agent-gotchas-and-limitations
section.p: Meta's automation and AI systems interact with agent-driven creative workflows in unexpected ways. Awareness of these pitfalls prevents silent failures.

antipattern.description: Meta's Standard Enhancements automatically rewrites copy and adds music to videos in 2024+ accounts.
antipattern.reason: The creative variant your agent produced may not be the one served. If brand voice is critical, disable Standard Enhancements explicitly per ad creative. Agents must validate that disabled accounts stay disabled.

antipattern.description: AI-generated images are increasingly detected and de-prioritized by Meta's systems.
antipattern.reason: Advantage+ creative downranks "synthetic" assets when it can identify them. Human-shot UGC and professional photography outperform AI-generated visuals in delivery priority.

antipattern.description: Video creative without captions or with auto-generated captions that are unverified.
antipattern.reason: 85% of users watch video on mute. Uncaptioned video is wasted spend. Agents rendering "audio-on by default" videos and skipping caption burn-in lose most of the audience.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated copy has distinctive tells: em-dashes, "in today's fast-paced world", "leverage", "robust", "seamless", "elevate".
antipattern.reason: Trained readers (both humans and Meta's detection systems) flag this as AI-generated, reducing trust and engagement. Agents should lint output for these phrases and rewrite.

antipattern.description: Agents generating 10 variants that repeat the same hook with minor word changes.
antipattern.reason: Embedding-similarity checks before upload catch duplicates. Testing 10 variants that are actually 3 variants repeated fails to isolate variables and wastes budget.

antipattern.description: Image models hallucinating text on images that gets read as gibberish.
antipattern.reason: Agents should render text overlays in code (PIL/CSS), not in the image generation prompt. Rendered text is legible; hallucinated text undermines trust.

antipattern.description: Video models failing captions or rendering them in wrong aspect.
antipattern.reason: Always re-caption with Whisper post-render and overlay programmatically. Do not trust the caption output from generation APIs.

rule.statement: Do not let an agent kill creatives at < 1000 impressions or < $20 spend — that is noise, not signal.
rule.rationale: High-variance outcomes below spend threshold look like poor performers but are often just unlucky. Killing below threshold introduces survivorship bias that kills decent creatives while keeping mediocre ones that started lucky.

rule.statement: First creative on a new product, any imagery showing people (model release / likeness rights), any claim mentioning numbers or competitors — human-in-loop review required before publishing.
rule.rationale: Agents should upload as PAUSED and await human eyeball-review before enabling. This prevents brand damage, legal risk, and policy violations that agents cannot predict.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Learned patterns that scale creative performance.

list.item: Always produce 1:1 AND 9:16 of every concept; Stories/Reels inventory is the cheapest CPM and you waste it without vertical assets
Burn captions into video — 85% watch on mute; uncaptioned video is wasted spend
Hook in the first 3 seconds (video) or first 7 words (image overlay). After that the user is gone
Refresh creative every 14 days even if metrics are still good — proactively avoids the cliff. CTR decay is exponential, not linear
Keep a "winner library" of past top performers tagged by angle/visual/format. Agents iterate on these, never start from scratch
For systematic A/B testing: change ONE variable per ad (hook OR visual OR copy) — multivariate inside a single ad set is uninterpretable

template.purpose: Image ad spec sheet: visual checklist, copy fields, tracking
